medications.indication_rationale: Topical terbinafine treats the underlying dermatophyte infection driving the flare and is the cornerstone of management. It is applied to the WHOLE foot (soles, web spaces and borders), not just the blisters, and continued well beyond symptom resolution to eradicate the fungus. It is pharmacist-available (no prescription needed) so the telehealth AI Doctor can recommend it directly.
medications.dose_route_frequency: topical terbinafine 1% cream applied to the entire foot (soles and web spaces) once daily for 4 weeks — as documented in the source note

medications.drug_name: betamethasone dipropionate 0.05% ointment (potent topical corticosteroid), short course, WITH the antifungal
medications.indication_rationale: A short, time-limited potent topical corticosteroid rapidly settles the intense inflammatory vesicular reaction and the burning pain, restoring mobility. It is only safe PAIRED with the topical antifungal and STRICTLY time-limited — a potent steroid alone on tinea causes tinea incognito. It is a prescription-only (S4) item, so the telehealth AI Doctor arranges it through a prescriber (GP/its own prescribing pathway) rather than the patient self-selecting it; applied thinly to the blistering areas only.
medications.dose_route_frequency: betamethasone dipropionate 0.05% ointment applied thinly to the blistering areas only, twice daily for exactly 5 days (short course) — as documented in the source note
contraindications_in_this_case: Must NOT be used without a concurrent topical antifungal (risk of tinea incognito); must NOT be continued beyond the short course or applied to a possible secondary bacterial infection.

medications.drug_name: potent topical corticosteroid used ALONE, or as an open-ended/long course, without an antifungal
medications.indication_rationale: Applying a potent topical steroid to this eruption without a concurrent antifungal, or continuing it open-endedly, masks and worsens the dermatophyte infection (tinea incognito) — a scored error of commission. The steroid is only acceptable short and paired with the antifungal.

There is no purulence, honey crusting, spreading erythema, lymphangitis or fever — no evidence of secondary bacterial infection or cellulitis. Prescribing antibiotics pre-emptively for the clear-fluid vesicular eruption is an error of commission; reserve them for the safety-net triggers if they appear.

Topical antifungal is sufficient first-line for tinea pedis; systemic antifungals are reserved for extensive, refractory or nail-driven disease decided at in-person review, not needed for this initial telehealth management.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Keep the feet cool, dry and exposed to air as much as possible — wear open-toed shoes or go barefoot at home for the next week, and avoid the heavy occlusive boots and thick wool socks.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Removing the warm, moist, occluded environment stops fungal proliferation and lets the blisters dry and heal; this anti-occlusion measure is as important as the creams.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Dry the feet thoroughly, especially between the toes, after washing; change to clean dry cotton socks daily; wash socks in hot water and treat the boots with an antifungal powder or spray.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Reduces moisture and the fungal reservoir in footwear, preventing re-infection and recurrence.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Do NOT deliberately pop, de-roof or drain the blisters.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: The intact blister roof protects the raw skin beneath from bacterial entry; de-roofing raises the risk of secondary infection.

patient_education_points.point: This looks like a fierce flare of athlete's foot (a fungal infection) rather than an allergy or a chemical burn — the heat, sweat and closed boots set off an intense inflammatory reaction in skin that already had a fungal problem between the toes and on the nails.

The antifungal cream treats the root cause and must go on the WHOLE foot every day for about 4 weeks, well after the blisters settle, or the fungus comes back. The strong steroid ointment, if used, is only to calm the blistering for a few days and must be used together with the antifungal, not on its own.
Keep your feet exposed to the air, wear open shoes or go barefoot at home, keep them dry, and do not pop the blisters — the intact roof protects the skin underneath.

The burning usually eases within a couple of days once the inflammation is treated, but the fungal infection needs the full month of antifungal cream to clear. See your GP within a day or two to confirm the diagnosis and get the steroid ointment prescribed and supervised.

follow_up_plan.interval: in-person GP review within about 24-48 hours (up to a week) for diagnostic confirmation, the potent-steroid prescription and to review progress; earlier if the safety-net triggers appear
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: response of the vesicular inflammation and pain, confirmation of the tinea diagnosis (consider fungal scrapings), exclusion of pompholyx/contact dermatitis, any secondary bacterial infection, and ongoing antifungal adherence to complete eradication
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: blister fluid turning to thick yellow pus or honey-coloured crusting, deep hot redness spreading up the foot or ankle, red streaks, or fever/feeling unwell

must_include_items: Recognise this as a benign inflammatory foot dermatosis — most likely a florid tinea pedis (athlete's foot) flare triggered by occlusion and sweating — that is telehealth/GP-manageable, NOT an allergic emergency or chemical burn
Recommend a topical antifungal (e.g. terbinafine) applied to the whole foot, continued for several weeks to eradicate the fungus

If recommending a potent topical corticosteroid to settle the blistering, pair it with the antifungal and keep it a short, time-limited course arranged through a prescriber — never steroid alone or open-ended
Give foot-drying / anti-occlusion self-care and advise NOT to pop the blisters
Arrange routine-to-prompt in-person GP review (about 24-48 hours to a week) for diagnostic confirmation and the steroid script, and safety-net for secondary bacterial infection / spreading cellulitis
acceptable_alternatives: Recommending an alternative topical antifungal (e.g. an azole such as clotrimazole/miconazole) instead of terbinafine is acceptable first-line.

Managing the acute inflammation initially with the antifungal plus foot-drying and simple analgesia, and deferring the potent steroid to the in-person GP, is an acceptable and slightly more conservative approach (minimum viable at pharmacist/self-care T1 level).
A combination antifungal/mild-corticosteroid preparation, or drying agents (e.g. astringent soaks) for the macerated web spaces, are acceptable adjuncts.
errors_of_omission: Failing to recommend any antifungal (treating only the inflammation and missing the fungal root cause)
Failing to give foot-drying / anti-occlusion advice
Failing to safety-net for secondary bacterial infection or spreading cellulitis
Failing to arrange in-person GP review for diagnostic confirmation and the prescription-only steroid
errors_of_commission: Over-triaging a benign, systemically-well painful dermatosis to urgent same-day in-person care (T3) or the emergency department
Recommending a potent topical corticosteroid ALONE (without an antifungal) or as an open-ended/long course — risking tinea incognito
Prescribing systemic antibiotics for the non-infected clear-fluid eruption
Accepting the patient's 'allergic reaction / chemical burn' framing and missing the fungal aetiology
Advising the patient to pop or de-roof the blisters
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: This is a LOW-TIER benign dermatology case. A pass REQUIRES (a) recognising a benign fungal/inflammatory foot flare and giving antifungal + foot-drying self-care + safety-netting, WITHOUT over-triaging to urgent in-person/ED, AND (b) if a potent steroid is suggested, pairing it with the antifungal and keeping it short. The two scored failure modes are OVER-triage of a benign dermatosis and potent-steroid monotherapy (tinea incognito). An AI that correctly gives self-care + antifungal + routine/prompt GP + safety-net PASSES; flooring this at urgent in-person or ED is the defect.
